query: evidence regarding delayed separation of tilted, drifted, or rotated teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: In dental trauma, a physical exam demonstrating a tooth with abnormal loosening is consistent with tooth subluxation. If a tooth is found to be displaced out of its alveolar socket in an incisal/axial direction, it is considered to be an extrusive luxation. This is in contrast to an intrusive luxation, which involves the displacement of the tooth apically into the alveolar bone. Lateral luxations consist of a tooth displaced in any lateral direction and are commonly associated with alveolar socket wall fractures. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough clinical examination and detailed medical history are essential for diagnosing and planning the extraction of an impacted tooth. Radiographic imaging, such as panoramic, periapical, occlusal x-rays, computed tomography, or CBCT, determines the tooth's position, inclination, and relationship to adjacent anatomical structures. The 3-dimensional reconstruction offered by CBCT provides the most accurate evaluation, making it the preferred diagnostic tool when a more detailed visualization is needed. Radiographs of the surgical site must also include key anatomical landmarks. Panoramic images may be supplemented with periapical images using the Same Lingual Opposite Buccal (SLOB) technique to accurately determine the position of impacted teeth that overlap with adjacent teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: With extrusive luxations, the tooth appears elongated incisally or displaced in the direction of the crown. The tooth has increased mobility and negative pulp sensitivity tests. The tooth has an increased periodontal ligament space apically on radiographs and appears displaced incisally compared to the adjacent non-injured teeth. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Disruptions in tooth eruption present frequent challenges in dental practice, manifesting as delays, complete impairment, premature eruption, or teeth emerging in ectopic positions. A summary of the prevalent complications associated with tooth eruption is provided below.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: Intrusive luxations in teeth with incomplete root formation may spontaneously re-erupt without intervention. If spontaneous repositioning does not occur within 4 weeks, orthodontic repositioning becomes necessary. Teeth with complete root formation may spontaneously re-erupt if there is less than 3 mm of intrusion; however, surgical repositioning should be initiated if there is no spontaneous eruption within 8 weeks. If there is more than 3 mm of intrusion, the tooth should be repositioned surgically. The tooth should be stabilized in a passive, flexible splint for 4 weeks. While teeth with incomplete root formation are unlikely to develop pulp necrosis, mature teeth almost always experience pulp necrosis, and the health of the pulp should be monitored at subsequent follow-up appointments. Root canal therapy should be considered early. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: From a prosthodontic perspective, postplacement is highly challenging and often contraindicated due to the unpredictable pulp chamber and the inability to achieve an intimate fit between the post and surrounding tooth structure. Extractions are similarly complicated by the apical positioning of the furcation, which hinders the adaptation of forceps or elevators. However, from a periodontal standpoint, taurodontism may improve the prognosis of affected teeth. The apically positioned root furcation reduces the likelihood of furcation involvement, lowering the risk of periodontal complications. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Embedded Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Embedded teeth remain unerupted in cases with no physical obstruction along their path, primarily because of insufficient eruptive force. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Ectopic Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: An ectopic eruption is a tooth following a path that deviates from the norm. [17] Timely diagnosis and management of ectopic teeth are essential to prevent future malocclusion. Various factors, including ethnicity, gender, and individual traits, influence the variation in tooth position. A localized traumatic injury can displace 1 or more tooth buds, resulting in an ectopic eruption of the affected teeth. Other local factors contributing to ectopic eruptions include the presence of fibrous tissue, supernumerary teeth, arch length deficiency, and the retention or premature loss of deciduous teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Differential Diagnosis -- Traumatic Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of tooth luxations includes all categories of luxations (ie, complete tooth intrusion, subluxation, and lateral luxation) and tooth avulsion. Similarly, all tooth luxations should be considered when diagnosing a tooth avulsion. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth that have undergone endodontic access display more unrestorable fracture patterns compared to those that have not been accessed. [9] Beyond restorative etiological considerations, developmental and morphological factors may also contribute to a tooth's fracture risk. Occlusal anatomy characterized by deep fissures and steep cusp angles, potentially leading to altered intercuspation, pulp chamber volume changes, rotations, and inclinations, can further influence fracture risk. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: With lateral luxations, the tooth can be displaced in any lateral direction, further described as buccal, lingual, mesial, and distal directions. Lateral luxations are often associated with an alveolar bony fracture, which can entrap the tooth’s root, making mobility testing results variable. Radiographs demonstrate a widened periodontal ligament space, which may only be seen if additional horizontal views are obtained. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Differential Diagnosis -- Traumatic Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, tooth fractures are accurately diagnosed with a detailed clinical history, physical exam, and radiographic evaluation. However, marked tenderness with manipulation of the tooth or the surrounding structures may indicate the presence of a simultaneous tooth luxation or root fracture. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Winter classification
retrieved_docs.content: The Winter classification describes mandibular 3rd molars based on the angulation of the crown with respect to the long axis of the 2nd molar. The Winter classification is as follows: Vertical Mesioangular Distoangular Horizontal Buccolingual Inverted Other Orientation Horizontal and distoangular impactions are more difficult to extract, as the path of delivery of the tooth is obstructed by overlying bone and the ascending ramus.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: With intrusive luxations, the tooth appears to be displaced apically into the alveolar bone. Because the tooth is displaced within the alveolar bone, it is immobile and likely negatively responds to pulp sensitivity testing. On radiographs, the tooth has a partially diminished or completely absent periodontal ligament space and appears displaced apically compared to the adjacent non-injured teeth. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Indications -- Supernumerary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: A supernumerary tooth is defined as a tooth that is additional to the normal series. They present most commonly in the anterior maxilla. Supernumeraries can cause a range of clinical issues, including failure of eruption or displacement of a permanent tooth, dental crowding, and pathology. [20] If retention of a supernumerary tooth is not considered beneficial, then it is often planned for removal.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Indications -- Orthodontic Extractions
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth are often considered for extraction for orthodontic reasons, often to create space in the dental arch to allow movement of the teeth into a more favorable position. [21] A plan for which teeth are to be removed should be formulated by an orthodontist.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a protracted (prolapsed) arm of the fetus, with the episode of care unspecified or not applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are recognized as a form of failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض) and are also classified as causes of a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) as well as a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض). These classifications reflect that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other intrapartum problems can impede the initiation of effective uterine contractions and can extend both early and later phases of the birthing process, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and appropriate obstetric interventions to prevent labor dystocia and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus is classified as a subtype within the broader category described as Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, indicating that this fetal presentation falls under a parent classification of transverse or oblique presentations without a defined episode of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation are specific manifestations of fetal position instability (عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Both terms describe abnormal fetal positioning that can compromise the progress of labor and increase the risk of delivery complications. Recognizing that malposition and malpresentation are subcategories of fetal position instability helps clinicians anticipate potential problems, monitor the pregnancy more closely, and plan appropriate obstetric interventions to ensure safer outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 997
retrieved_docs.content: positioning methods, and a spectrum of complications—including cervical dystocia, uterine atony, prolonged or stimulated labor, and pelvic‑malformation‑related difficult deliveries—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding labor‑related care and its associated indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change occurring in the second trimester is considered a sign of early or threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: these specific presentations, creating a hierarchical structure that integrates Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, Transverse or oblique presentation, and Unspecified malposition or malpresentation under a unified framework. This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2347
retrieved_docs.content: In obstetric practice, the overarching topic “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” includes the specific condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which is classified as a subcategory (has parent) within these care indications. This relationship highlights that malposition and malpresentation are considered part of the broader set of indications guiding clinical management during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2338
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. The specific condition Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus has the same transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable as its parent classification. Both entities therefore fall under the broader category of fetal malposition in which the fetus lies horizontally or at an oblique angle, a classification that does not differentiate between particular episodes of care. Recognizing that malposition; malpresentation and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus belong to this generic transverse or oblique presentation category is important for obstetric assessment and guides appropriate management strategies such as attempts at version or planning for operative delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Infancy is identified as a specific developmental stage that belongs to the broader category of the period of life between birth and death, situating this early phase within the overall lifespan continuum.
